sections.title: Market Organization and Structure
sections.content: Financial markets facilitate the exchange of securities. Understanding market structures, order types, and trading mechanisms is essential for equity investing.
keyPoints: Market orders execute at current market price
Limit orders execute at specified price or better

practiceQuestions.question: In which market are new securities issued to investors?
practiceQuestions.explanation: The primary market is where new securities are issued and sold to investors for the first time (e.g., IPOs).

practiceQuestions.question: A limit order to buy will execute:
options: At the specified price or lower
At the specified price or higher
practiceQuestions.explanation: A limit buy order specifies the maximum price the buyer is willing to pay. It will execute at that price or lower.

sections.title: Security Market Indices
sections.content: Market indices track the performance of a group of securities representing a particular market or sector. They serve as benchmarks and investment vehicles.
keyPoints: Price-weighted: higher-priced stocks have more weight
Market-cap weighted: larger companies have more weight
Equal-weighted: all stocks have same weight

practiceQuestions.question: The Dow Jones Industrial Average is what type of index?
practiceQuestions.explanation: The DJIA is a price-weighted index, meaning higher-priced stocks have a greater impact on the index value.

practiceQuestions.question: Which index weighting method gives the same importance to each stock regardless of size?
practiceQuestions.explanation: Equal-weighted indices assign the same weight to each constituent stock, regardless of market capitalization or price.

sections.title: Equity Valuation
sections.content: Equity valuation methods estimate the intrinsic value of stocks. Common approaches include DDM, DCF, and valuation multiples.
keyPoints: DDM values stock based on expected dividends
P/E ratio compares price to earnings
P/B ratio compares price to book value

formulas.expression: P/E Ratio = Price / Earnings per Share

practiceQuestions.question: Using the Gordon Growth Model, if D₁ = $2, r = 10%, and g = 5%, the intrinsic value is:
A stock trading at $50 with EPS of $5 has a P/E ratio of:
